faqItems.a: Acreage and views come with responsibilities. Lot maintenance, well/septic considerations in some areas, and the reality of drought and water restrictions can matter more than people expect.

faqItems.q: 4) How do I pick the right area if I’m relocating?
faqItems.a: Anchor the decision on your weekday. Where are you commuting, what errands do you run, and what do you do after work? Then we match that routine to a pocket, and only then start filtering homes.

faqItems.q: 5) Can you help me compare neighborhoods without wasting weekends touring?
faqItems.a: Yes. I’ll start with a quick call to map your priorities, then I’ll shortlist pockets and neighborhoods that fit your routine, budget, and must-haves so your tours are high-signal instead of exhausting.

title: New Braunfels Home Pricing Strategy (2026)
excerpt: New Braunfels home pricing strategy in 2026 starts with today’s comps, not last year’s headlines. Here’s how to price, position, and negotiate to protect your bottom line.
content:
How do you price your New Braunfels home in 2026 without leaving money on the table or watching it sit?

Quick Answer

In 2026, the best New Braunfels pricing strategy is simple but disciplined: price to the most recent comparable sales in your specific pocket, launch with strong presentation, and go in with a clear plan for concessions. Start with a real comp-driven valuation (not a guess) and sanity-check your net using a seller’s net sheet. If you want a second set of eyes on your price range, I can run a hyperlocal valuation through my home value process.

The Complete Picture

If you’re thinking about selling in New Braunfels this year, you’ve probably felt the shift: buyers are still out there, but they’re more selective, more payment-sensitive, and more likely to compare your home to every other option in a few clicks.

That’s exactly why pricing strategy matters more in 2026 than it did during the frenzy years. The goal is not to “win the list price conversation.” The goal is to create a launch price and positioning that:

  • earns serious showings quickly
  • protects your leverage in negotiation
  • keeps your contract clean through inspection and appraisal
  • nets you the most money with the least drama

Here’s the part most sellers miss: New Braunfels isn’t one market. It’s a collection of pockets.

A home near downtown/Landa Park, a newer build in Veramendi, a neighborhood on the south side, and a Hill Country property out toward Canyon Lake can all move at different speeds, with different buyer profiles and different “deal” expectations. So when someone says, “the market is doing X,” the real question is, “in your pocket, at your price point, with your condition and layout, what are buyers choosing today?”

What “Strategic Pricing” Actually Means in 2026

Strategic pricing is not just a number. It’s a plan.

A strong pricing plan has three layers:

  • The data layer: recent closed comps, pending comps, and the actives you’re truly competing against.
  • The product layer: how your condition, updates, layout, lot, and location compare to those comps.
  • The leverage layer: how you’ll respond if you get multiple offers, a single offer, or crickets.

In 2026, I’m watching many markets show a higher share of price reductions and more time on market than the hyper-competitive years, which lines up with broader national data sources. That doesn’t mean “prices are crashing.” It means buyers have more options, and the market is less forgiving when a listing is priced based on hope instead of evidence.

Key Insights (with local examples)

  • Your first week is your best shot at top-dollar interest. In New Braunfels, the homes that sell fastest tend to feel “obviously well-positioned” the moment they hit the market: clean, bright photos, clear showing access, and a price that makes sense next to the most recent closes.

  • Comps do not equal comps unless the buyer would actually swap. If your home backs to a busy road, has a unique floor plan, or hasn’t been updated in a while, the true comp set narrows. A strategic price reflects the homes a buyer would actually tour instead of yours.

  • Concessions are a tool, not a defeat. With rates influencing monthly payments, a credit toward closing costs or a targeted rate buydown can sometimes unlock a better buyer pool than simply shaving the price. The key is to decide in advance how you want to use concessions so you don’t negotiate emotionally.

  • Online estimates are a starting point, not a pricing strategy. Automated values struggle in pockets where one street sells differently than the next (because of schools, traffic patterns, lot type, amenities, or newer vs. older construction). A true pricing strategy is built from the most similar recent sales and what’s currently competing.

  • A price reduction is not “bad.” A late price reduction is expensive. When a listing sits, buyers start asking, “what’s wrong with it?” If we need to adjust, doing it early and intentionally is usually far less costly than chasing the market after weeks of low activity.

The Market Reality: What the Numbers Mean (and what they don’t)

When you read headlines about inventory, days on market, and price cuts, they’re useful as a backdrop. They help explain why buyers behave differently now than they did when rates were lower and choices were fewer.

But the mistake is using broad stats to price a specific home.

Here’s the way I translate the “big picture” into an actual pricing plan for a New Braunfels seller:

  • Inventory up: buyers will compare more homes. Your home has to win on price, condition, or terms (ideally two of the three).
  • Days on market higher than the frenzy years: buyers don’t feel as rushed, so presentation and first impressions matter even more.
  • Price reductions more common: the market is signaling that some sellers are starting too high and adjusting later.

The right response is not fear. It’s clarity.

Pricing strategically means you choose a price that makes sense for today’s buyer, and you choose it with your eyes open.

That includes being honest about the “math” of the deal. Before we ever choose a list price, I like sellers to look at expected net proceeds and decide what matters most: maximum price, clean terms, closing timeline, or certainty.

This is where a seller’s net sheet is incredibly helpful, because it turns pricing into a decision you can feel confident about instead of a guess you hope works out.

The Pricing Framework I Use for New Braunfels Sellers

When I build a pricing strategy with a seller, I’m typically pressure-testing three price lanes:

  • Market lane: priced directly in line with recent comps, designed to attract steady activity and a normal negotiation.
  • Momentum lane: priced to encourage faster action (sometimes useful if you want a quicker timeline or you’re competing with other listings).
  • Premium lane (only when justified): used when the home truly has something scarce that the comps don’t capture, like a very hard-to-find location, view, lot, or finish level.

What I don’t like doing is the “wish lane,” where the home is priced higher because it would be nice if someone paid it.

In 2026, buyers are usually too informed for that. They’ll simply move on to the next option, and by the time you adjust, you’ve lost the advantage of being new on the market.

Action Steps: A practical seller checklist (before you pick a price)

If you want to sell with confidence, do these items before you get emotionally attached to a number:

  • Get the right comp set. Use recent closed sales that match your pocket, size, condition, and features. If you’re not sure what counts as “similar,” that’s exactly what I help with.
  • Be brutally honest about condition. Deferred maintenance shows up in buyer objections and inspection negotiations. If it’s fixable, fix it. If it’s not, price for it.
  • Decide what you want from the sale. Maximum price, speed, or certainty. You can usually pick two.
  • Plan your concession strategy in advance. Decide what you would offer (if anything) for closing costs, repairs, or rate buydowns so you’re not making decisions under pressure.
  • Prepare your “first-week” launch. Photos, showing access, and a clean presentation matter because your first week is when you’re most visible.
  • Know your bottom line. Run a net sheet so you understand how price, concessions, and fees translate to your proceeds.

If you want, I can run a detailed valuation through my home value process and show you the comp set, the realistic price lanes, and the most likely negotiation points for your pocket.

Timing and launch: when strategy beats “the perfect week”

Sellers ask me all the time, “When’s the best time to list?” And yes, seasonality is real. Spring and early summer usually bring more buyer activity in New Braunfels, and that can help well-positioned homes.

But in 2026, the bigger lever is often how you launch, not whether you list on the “perfect” week.

A strategic launch looks like this:

  • You list when you can show the home at its best (clean, bright, minimal deferred maintenance, photos that match reality).
  • You pick a pricing lane that makes sense for your pocket and your buyer pool.
  • You are ready for the first week of activity, because that’s when serious buyers decide whether you’re a must-see or a maybe-later.

If your schedule forces a less-than-ideal week, that’s okay. The way we price and position can still create urgency. On the flip side, listing in a “hot” week doesn’t fix an overpriced home or one that presents poorly.

One practical tip: before we go live, I like to walk the home and ask, “What would a buyer object to in the first five minutes?” Smell, lighting, worn paint, visible patchwork, a cluttered garage, a yard that looks stressed. These are small things, but they influence how buyers feel about your price.

Negotiation: How to keep more of your money (even when buyers ask)

Most sellers think negotiation is about standing firm. In reality, negotiation is about choosing the trade that benefits you most.

If a buyer asks for repairs, a credit, or help with closing costs, the best move depends on why the buyer is asking and what it does to your net and your timeline.

A few negotiation principles I use with sellers:

  • Protect appraisal risk. If we accept an offer that’s aggressive on price, we need a plan for the appraisal conversation. Sometimes a slightly lower offer with stronger terms can be the safer path to closing.
  • Separate “real” issues from preference issues. Some inspection items are safety or function problems. Others are upgrades the buyer wants you to pay for. Your response should match the category.
  • Use credits strategically. Credits can keep the headline price intact (useful for appraisal optics) while still solving a buyer’s payment problem.
  • Keep your timeline in mind. The longer a deal drags, the more likely it is to break. Clean timelines often net more because they reduce your risk.

The sellers who do best in 2026 usually aren’t the ones who refuse everything. They’re the ones who pick the right “yes,” and say it in a way that still protects their leverage.

Closing CTA

If you’re selling in New Braunfels or the surrounding Hill Country and you want a clear plan (not just a guess), I’m happy to help. I’ll show you the most relevant comps for your pocket, build pricing lanes, and map out a negotiation strategy that fits your goals.

Reach out anytime here: Cody Posey Real Estate, or call/text me at 830.360.5569.

Sources

  • Realtor.com Research — March 2026 Monthly Housing Report: https://www.realtor.com/research/march-2026-data/
  • Freddie Mac PMMS — Mortgage Rates Continue to Decline (Apr 16, 2026): https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/04/16/3275689/0/en/Mortgage-Rates-Continue-to-Decline.html
  • National Association of REALTORS® — Existing-Home Sales: https://www.nar.realtor/research-and-statistics/housing-statistics/existing-home-sales
  • San Antonio Report — Will San Antonio's housing market tip toward buyers or sellers?: https://sanantonioreport.org/san-antonio-housing-market-2026-buyers-sellers/

Sources: Realtor.com Research — March 2026 Monthly Housing Report; Freddie Mac PMMS — Mortgage Rates Continue to Decline (Apr 16, 2026); National Association of REALTORS® — Existing-Home Sales; San Antonio Report — Will San Antonio's housing market tip toward buyers or sellers?.

faqItems.q: 1) Is it better to list high and negotiate down?
faqItems.a: Usually no. In most pockets, overpricing costs you the ‘new listing’ momentum and invites price reductions later, which can weaken your negotiating position. A sharper, comp-based price up front tends to produce better traffic and cleaner offers.

faqItems.q: 2) What comps matter most when pricing a home in New Braunfels?
faqItems.a: Recent closed sales that match your home’s location, size, condition, and amenities are the foundation. Pending sales and active listings add context, but closings show what buyers actually paid for comparable options.

faqItems.q: 3) Are seller concessions normal in 2026?
faqItems.a: In many transactions, yes. The question is not whether concessions exist, it’s whether you use them strategically: targeted credits or buydowns can sometimes net better than a straight price cut, depending on your buyer pool.

faqItems.q: 4) How long should I wait before adjusting the price?
faqItems.a: If showings and saves are low in the first week, that’s a signal to re-check price and presentation quickly. If you’re getting showings but no offers, the issue is often price, terms, or condition objections you can address.

faqItems.q: 5) What’s the biggest pricing mistake you see sellers make?
faqItems.a: Using broad headlines or online estimates instead of hyperlocal data. In New Braunfels, two nearby neighborhoods can behave like two different markets, so ‘average’ stats can mislead your pricing by a lot.

title: Gruene New Braunfels Real Estate: Neighborhood Spotlight
excerpt: Looking at Gruene in New Braunfels? Here’s what to know about home prices, the lifestyle, schools, and why buyers keep choosing this iconic Hill Country pocket.
content:
Thinking about living near Gruene in New Braunfels, but unsure what it really costs and what the day-to-day feels like?

Quick Answer

Gruene is a lifestyle-driven slice of New Braunfels real estate where pricing depends heavily on the exact pocket (walkability to the Historic District and river proximity matter). If you want to see what’s available right now, start with a focused search on my site: view homes for sale. Then we can narrow it down with true neighborhood-level comps for the streets you’re considering.

The Complete Picture

If you’ve spent even one Saturday in the Gruene Historic District, you already understand why this area stays on so many buyers’ short lists. It’s not just a place on the map, it’s a feeling: live music drifting out of an old dance hall, a river breeze at sunset, and that very Texas mix of history, boots, and Hill Country charm.

But when it comes time to actually buy a home, “Gruene” can be a little confusing, because it doesn’t behave like a perfectly boxed-in subdivision. People use the word to describe the Historic District itself and several nearby residential pockets. Two homes can both be described as “near Gruene” and still have very different pricing, school zoning, traffic patterns, and resale behavior.

So in this neighborhood spotlight, I want to do two things:

  • Give you the lifestyle and location context that makes Gruene, well, Gruene.
  • Help you think about pricing and strategy the right way in 2026, without pretending there’s one neat “median price” that applies to every street.

What “living in Gruene” usually means

Most buyers mean one (or more) of these when they say “I want Gruene”:

  • Walkability or quick access to the Historic District (music, dining, shops).
  • Close proximity to the Guadalupe River (or the overall “river town” vibe).
  • Easy access to the south/east side of New Braunfels, where you can still get around town quickly.
  • A neighborhood feel that’s a little more character-driven than some of the newer, more uniform master-planned areas.

That’s why the right question isn’t “What’s the price of Gruene?” It’s:

  • Which pocket is actually the best fit for my day-to-day?
  • What are the best comparable sales for that pocket in the last 3 to 6 months?
  • What’s competing with it right now (including new construction and resale inventory)?

Key Insights (what I see buyers miss)

  • Gruene is lifestyle-first. The “premium” (when there is one) usually comes from proximity and experience, not just square footage.
  • Micro-markets matter here. A home that’s five minutes closer to the Historic District can attract a different buyer than one that’s five minutes farther out, even if the floorplan is similar.
  • In 2026, buyers are paying attention to condition and pricing discipline. Turnkey homes that are priced correctly can still move, while homes that feel “aspirational” on price can sit.
  • School zoning is not one-size-fits-all around New Braunfels, and “near Gruene” does not automatically mean the same school district.

Lifestyle: why people fall in love with Gruene

Gruene’s story is tied to the Historic District and its reputation as a destination for live music and old-school Texas energy. Gruene Hall, for example, notes it was built in 1878 and is known as Texas’ oldest continually operating dance hall (gruenehall.com). That’s not just trivia, it’s part of what makes this area feel different from a typical suburban pocket.

What buyers tell me they want from Gruene usually sounds like this:

  • “I want to be close to the action, but not in the middle of it.”
  • “I want character. I don’t want every street to feel identical.”
  • “I want to be able to host friends and have options nearby.”

The best way to know if Gruene fits your lifestyle is to visit like a local, not like a tourist.

Do this before you buy (simple, but it works)

  • Go once on a weekday evening.
  • Go once on a weekend afternoon.
  • Drive the route you’ll take to work, school, the grocery store, and the gym.
  • Pay attention to traffic patterns, parking, and noise levels.

That one extra hour of “real life” recon usually saves buyers from the classic regret of loving a vibe on a Saturday, then realizing they don’t love the logistics on a Tuesday.

Schools: how to think about it without guessing

School conversations can get messy fast, especially online. Here’s the clean approach:

  • New Braunfels has multiple districts in play across the area.
  • “Gruene” is a label that can cover multiple pockets.
  • Zoning can change, and boundaries can be more nuanced than most people realize.

So I don’t recommend a school district in a vacuum. I help you identify the school assignments for the exact home you’re considering, and I point you to the official district resources so you can evaluate what matters for your family.

If you want a helpful starting point for New Braunfels resources on the site, I keep a dedicated page here: New Braunfels schools.

(For broader reference, Texas Tribune’s Schools Explorer offers a detailed profile for New Braunfels ISD, and Comal ISD publishes district overview information as well. You’ll see both linked in the sources.)

Market Reality (2026): what the numbers mean, and what they don’t

Let’s talk about pricing without oversimplifying it.

Public dashboards can be useful for setting expectations about the overall direction of the market. For example, Redfin’s New Braunfels dashboard showed a February 2026 median sale price around $307,000 (about -5.5% year over year) (redfin.com). Zillow also publishes a New Braunfels snapshot with additional macro indicators (zillow.com).

Here’s the key point: those numbers are citywide and can move because the mix of what sold changed.

If one month has more builder closings at the entry level, or fewer high-end resales, the “median” can shift even if your target neighborhood is stable. That’s why I’m careful not to treat a citywide chart as if it’s the answer for a specific street near Gruene.

How I translate “macro” into “micro” (the way buyers actually win)

When we get serious about a pocket near Gruene, I’m looking at:

  • Recent comparable sales that match your target home’s size, lot, age, and condition.
  • Active listings that are true competitors (not just anything within a zip code).
  • How pricing and condition interact (a perfectly updated home can live in a different demand tier).
  • Seller concessions, rate buydowns, and negotiation patterns, which matter more in a shifting market.

In plain English: the strategy isn’t “buy Gruene.” The strategy is “buy the right pocket near Gruene at the right number, with the right terms.”

Growth trends: why demand keeps showing up around New Braunfels

Buyers don’t move to Gruene in isolation. They move to New Braunfels and the Hill Country for a bigger set of reasons: lifestyle, jobs, family, proximity to San Antonio and Austin, and the simple fact that people keep relocating here.

Comal County’s growth is one of the biggest tailwinds in the area.

  • Community Impact reported Comal County saw a 27.8% population increase from 2020 to 2025 (communityimpact.com).
  • USAFacts also tracks Comal County population change and highlights recent gains (usafacts.org).

When an area is growing like that, it creates two realities at the same time:

  • More demand, more options, and more amenities over time.
  • More pressure on infrastructure, traffic, and school capacity, which means buyers need to think in specifics, not just vibes.

That’s another reason the “visit on different days” advice matters. Growth shows up in the day-to-day.

Who Gruene tends to fit best (and who should think twice)

Gruene is a great fit if you want

  • Lifestyle access: music, dining, and the Historic District energy.
  • A place that feels like it has a story.
  • A home base that’s close to river recreation and still connected to the rest of town.

You might think twice if you need

  • Total quiet and zero weekend traffic.
  • A highly uniform neighborhood where every home looks and feels similar.
  • A “set it and forget it” pricing model (Gruene-adjacent pockets require a little more micro-level attention).

None of these are dealbreakers. They’re just the tradeoffs that help you choose confidently.

A practical way to start your Gruene home search

If you’re early in the process, the simplest path is:

  • Identify the 2 to 3 pockets that match your lifestyle.
  • Look at what’s active today (and what’s been sitting).
  • Then pull comps for the exact pocket to understand where the market is actually clearing.

If you want, I can set you up with a search that’s tighter than the usual “New Braunfels” filter so you’re not sifting through hundreds of listings that don’t match what you mean by “Gruene.”

Closing CTA

If you’re considering Gruene (or “near Gruene”) and you want a clear, comps-based plan for what to pay and how to negotiate in today’s market, I’m happy to help. Reach out here: Cody Posey Real Estate or call/text 830.360.5569.

Sources

  • Redfin (New Braunfels housing market dashboard): https://www.redfin.com/city/13081/TX/New-Braunfels
  • Zillow (New Braunfels home values snapshot): https://www.zillow.com/home-values/16226/new-braunfels-tx/
  • USAFacts (Comal County population change): https://usafacts.org/answers/is-the-population-growing-or-shrinking/county/comal-county-tx/
  • Community Impact (Comal County population growth, 2020–2025): https://communityimpact.com/san-antonio/northeast-san-antonio-metrocom/government/2026/04/07/comal-county-sees-278-population-increase-from-2020-25/
  • Texas Tribune Schools Explorer (New Braunfels ISD profile): https://schools.texastribune.org/districts/new-braunfels-isd/
  • Comal ISD (district overview): https://www.comalisd.org
  • Gruene Hall (history): https://gruenehall.com
  • Texas Real Estate Research Center (Texas Housing Insight, March 2026): https://trerc.tamu.edu/reports/texas-housing-insight-march-2026/

Sources: Redfin (New Braunfels housing market dashboard); Zillow (New Braunfels home values snapshot); USAFacts (Comal County population change); Community Impact (Comal County population growth, 2020–2025); Texas Tribune Schools Explorer (New Braunfels ISD profile); Comal ISD (district overview); Gruene Hall (history); Texas Real Estate Research Center (Texas Housing Insight, March 2026).

faqItems.q: 1) Is Gruene a separate city from New Braunfels?
faqItems.a: Gruene is a historic district area within New Braunfels, and people use “Gruene” to describe several nearby pockets. When you’re buying, the exact address matters more than the label.

faqItems.q: 2) What do homes cost near Gruene?
faqItems.a: Expect a wide range. Some homes price like “typical New Braunfels,” while others command a premium for proximity to the Historic District, Guadalupe River access, and overall character. The only reliable way to know is to pull comps for the exact pocket and compare current competition.

faqItems.q: 3) What school district is Gruene in?
faqItems.a: It depends on the specific pocket and address. Parts of the greater New Braunfels area feed into either New Braunfels ISD or Comal ISD, so I always recommend verifying zoning for the home you’re considering.

faqItems.q: 4) Is Gruene good for commuters?
faqItems.a: For many buyers, yes. Gruene sits with easy access back into New Braunfels and toward IH-35 for commuting north or south. Your commute time will depend on which side of town you land on and your daily drive pattern.

faqItems.q: 5) Is Gruene more “touristy” than other neighborhoods?
faqItems.a: The Historic District itself draws visitors for music, shopping, and dining, especially on weekends. Many nearby residential streets still feel quiet, but it’s smart to visit at different times of day to get a feel for traffic, noise, and parking patterns.

title: Hill Country home buying plan (New Braunfels)
excerpt: Hill Country home buying starts with a plan. Here’s the step-by-step roadmap I use with New Braunfels buyers to finance smart, inspect fast, and offer with confidence.
content:
Does buying in the Hill Country feel like you only get one shot to get it right?

Quick Answer

You can absolutely buy with confidence, even in a shifting market, if you follow a simple order of operations: get fully pre-approved (not just pre-qualified), tour with a checklist, write a clean offer backed by facts, then use the Texas option period to inspect and negotiate quickly. If you want the full step-by-step, start with my home buying process guide.

The Complete Picture

Buying a home in the Texas Hill Country is exciting, but it’s also one of the biggest financial decisions you’ll make. And in and around New Braunfels, the experience can change a lot depending on the neighborhood, the price point, and even the week.

Some homes sit and give you room to negotiate. Others, especially the ones that are priced right and show well, still move quickly.

That’s why I coach buyers to stop thinking in terms of “house hunting” and start thinking in terms of a repeatable plan. When you follow the right order of operations, you avoid the two expensive mistakes I see most often:

  • Falling in love with a home before your numbers are locked in.
  • Rushing contract decisions without a due diligence game plan.

I’ll walk you through the exact roadmap I use with buyers so you feel prepared from financing all the way to the final walkthrough.

The Roadmap I Use With Buyers (Offer to Keys)

1) Start with real numbers, not vibes

Before we talk about paint colors or pools, we get your budget grounded in reality.

That means:

  • A lender who can explain your payment range clearly (principal, interest, taxes, insurance, HOA).
  • A pre-approval that accounts for what you actually want to buy, not a generic max.
  • A plan for your cash needs, including appraisal gaps (if any), option fee, earnest money, and closing costs.

If you’re still figuring out what “comfortable” looks like, my mortgage calculator is a good first step to sanity-check payments before you tour.

One more thing I’ll add, because it matters in 2026: we also talk about payment strategy. Some buyers want the lowest possible out-of-pocket today, some want the lowest possible payment, and some want flexibility.

Those decisions can change what “best” looks like:

  • A slightly higher purchase price with a seller credit could make sense if it lowers your cash-to-close or helps fund a rate buydown.
  • A clean, tighter offer might beat a higher offer that feels uncertain.
  • A payment you can comfortably handle for the next 3 to 5 years is usually more important than chasing the perfect interest rate headline.

My job is to help you pick a lane that fits your real life, not just the spreadsheet.

2) Tour homes like you’re verifying a claim

A good showing is not a vibe check, it’s a verification process.

When we tour, we’re not just asking “Do I like this?” We’re also asking:

  • Does the floorplan live the way you think it does, or does it just photograph well?
  • What will be expensive later (roof age, HVAC age, drainage patterns, big trees close to the foundation)?
  • What’s the neighborhood reality (traffic cut-throughs, noise, slope, access, nearby projects)?

This is where local experience matters. New Braunfels real estate can change block by block. The goal is to spot the “pockets” that match your lifestyle and avoid the ones that look good online but don’t fit day-to-day life.

When buyers tell me, “Everything looks the same after the fifth house,” that’s normal. What helps is using the same quick checklist every time so you can compare apples to apples:

  • Big-ticket ages: roof, HVAC, water heater (if you can see them)
  • Layout realities: bedroom sizes, storage, laundry placement, natural light
  • Exterior/drainage: slope toward the home, standing water, gutter discharge points
  • Street feel: traffic, parking, noise, and how it feels at different times of day
  • Future friction: HOA restrictions, weird easements, adjacency to commercial or cut-through roads

You don’t have to become a contractor to tour well. You just need a consistent way to notice patterns.

3) Write an offer that is strong and clear (not just high)

Most buyers assume “strong offer” equals “highest price.” Sometimes it does. Often it doesn’t.

In a multiple-offer situation, sellers typically choose the offer that feels most certain. Certainty can come from:

  • A clean timeline (fast enough to be attractive, realistic enough to actually close).
  • Solid proof of funds for down payment and any appraisal gap.
  • Reasonable contingencies that still protect you.
  • Thoughtful terms that reduce friction.

Here’s my rule: I’d rather help you submit one excellent offer on the right home than five rushed offers that expose you to risk.

And if you’re worried about overpaying, here’s the practical framework I use to keep buyers grounded:

  • Recent comparable sales tell us the realistic value range.
  • Active competition tells us the pressure level (how many similar homes are available right now).
  • Concessions tell us what the market is “really paying” (a $10,000 seller credit is effectively a different net price than a no-credit offer).

That combination helps us decide when to be aggressive, when to be patient, and when to walk.

4) Understand the Texas option period (your leverage window)

Texas is different than many other states because the contract has a negotiated termination option period. In plain English, it’s a short window of time after the contract’s Effective Date where you can terminate for any reason.

That’s why the option period is the heart of the buyer’s due diligence plan.

The practical takeaways:

  • The option period is counted in calendar days (not business days), and deadlines are strict.
  • The option fee is generally non-refundable, but it buys you flexibility.
  • This is when you have the most leverage to negotiate repairs or credits, because you can still walk away.

I’m careful here for a reason. Waiving protections you don’t fully understand is one of the fastest ways to turn a “great deal” into an expensive lesson.

If you’re relocating from out of state, this can feel unfamiliar. Many states use inspection contingencies with different rules. In Texas, the option period is the cleanest “pressure release valve” you have, which is why I plan around it from the very beginning.

5) Front-load inspections and due diligence

If you only remember one thing, remember this: don’t wait.

The best buyers treat day one of the option period like a sprint. We schedule:

  • General inspection immediately.
  • Specialty inspections only if the general report points to something material (foundation, roof, plumbing, drainage, etc.).

Hill Country properties can have unique “gotchas” that are very property-specific. Drainage is a big one. Flood risk is another. Even if a home isn’t in a floodplain, water can still move aggressively during storms.

A smart baseline check is FEMA’s Flood Map Service Center, then we combine that with on-the-ground reality and what the seller has disclosed.

If you’re buying in the Hill Country, there are a few scenarios where I’ll often recommend being extra thorough (depending on what the general inspection shows):

  • Older homes where plumbing lines, cast iron, or previous repairs are a question mark
  • Homes with noticeable slope, cracks, sticky doors, or other signs that call for a closer foundation look
  • Properties where drainage patterns could move water toward the home during heavy storms
  • Acreage properties where access, fencing, outbuildings, or utilities add complexity

The point is not to “find problems.” The point is to understand what you’re buying, what it might cost later, and what needs to be addressed now.

6) Negotiate like a grown-up (repairs, credits, and priorities)

The goal of an inspection is not to produce a “perfect” house. Every home has items.

A good negotiation focuses on:

  • Safety issues.
  • Big-ticket system failures.
  • Items that materially change the value or the risk profile.

Sometimes the right answer is repairs. Sometimes it’s a credit. Sometimes it’s a price adjustment. And sometimes it’s walking away because the risk no longer matches the deal.

This is also where the market reality shows up. In some pockets, sellers will address more. In others, they won’t. The difference is usually pricing, competition, and how unique the home is.

7) Keep the deal on track to closing

Once we’re past the option period, the job becomes execution.

That means staying ahead of:

  • Lender conditions (don’t let paperwork drag).
  • Appraisal timing.
  • Title and HOA documents.
  • Insurance and any required inspections.

A smooth close is usually the result of a boring, disciplined checklist.

If you want a deeper walkthrough of costs, timelines, and what to expect on the closing side, start with my buyer resources and then we’ll tailor it to your specific situation.

Key Insights (the “don’t mess this up” list)

  • The best buyer advantage is preparation. A fully pre-approved buyer can move quickly without panic.
  • A “winning offer” is usually a clean offer. Clear terms beat clever terms.
  • The option period is your leverage window in Texas. Use it intentionally.
  • Hill Country due diligence is property-specific. Drainage, grading, and water flow deserve real attention.
  • Your goal is not to win a bidding war. Your goal is to buy the right home on terms you can live with.

Market Reality in 2026 (what I’m seeing locally)

The market feels more nuanced than a simple “buyers market” or “sellers market.”

In New Braunfels and the surrounding Hill Country:

  • Well-priced, well-presented homes still get attention quickly.
  • Overpriced homes are sitting longer, and that creates negotiation opportunities.
  • Concessions (closing cost help, rate buydowns, repair credits) show up more often in certain price points.

This is exactly why a plan matters. You don’t want to treat every house the same, and you definitely don’t want to treat every neighborhood the same.

The buyers who do best right now usually share a few traits:

  • They’re decisive when the home matches the plan.
  • They’re patient when it doesn’t.
  • They don’t confuse “negotiation opportunity” with “automatic discount.”

In other words, we stay factual. We use comps, we use condition, we use current competition, and we keep the offer strategy tied to what the home is actually worth in today’s market.

That’s also why I like building a short “A-list” of neighborhoods or micro-areas for each buyer (the pockets that truly fit). It cuts down on decision fatigue and helps you recognize the right house faster when it shows up.

Action Steps: Your 7-Day Due Diligence Game Plan

If you’re going under contract, here’s a simple way to stay ahead of your deadlines.

  • Day 0: Contract executed. Confirm timelines and delivery requirements.
  • Day 1: Schedule inspection(s). Request seller disclosures and key docs.
  • Day 2–3: Inspection happens. Start prioritizing issues.
  • Day 3–5: Get estimates on any big-ticket findings.
  • Day 5–6: Decide what to request (repairs, credits, price adjustment).
  • Day 6–7: Negotiate and make a final decision before your option deadline.

Closing CTA

If you’re planning a move in New Braunfels or anywhere around the Texas Hill Country, I’m happy to help you build a plan that fits your budget and your timeline, then execute it without the stress.

Reach out here: Cody Posey Real Estate or call/text 830.360.5569.

Sources

  • Texas Real Estate Research Center (Texas A&M) — Option Period Basics: https://trerc.tamu.edu/article/option-period-basics-2360/
  • Freddie Mac — Primary Mortgage Market Survey (PMMS): https://www.freddiemac.com/pmms
  • FEMA Flood Map Service Center: https://msc.fema.gov/portal/home

Sources: Texas Real Estate Research Center (Texas A&M) — Option Period Basics; Freddie Mac — Primary Mortgage Market Survey (PMMS); FEMA Flood Map Service Center.

faqItems.q: 1) What is the option period in Texas, and why does it matter so much?
faqItems.a: The option period is a negotiated number of calendar days after the contract’s Effective Date when you can terminate for any reason. It’s the cleanest window for inspections and due diligence, and it’s when buyers have the most leverage to renegotiate or walk away if the risk isn’t right.

faqItems.q: 2) What’s the difference between earnest money and the option fee?
faqItems.a: Earnest money is a deposit held by the title company that generally applies to your purchase at closing and is typically refundable if you terminate properly under the contract (including within the option period). The option fee is paid as consideration for the option period and is generally non-refundable.

faqItems.q: 3) How fast do I need to schedule inspections in the Hill Country?
faqItems.a: As fast as possible. I recommend booking a general inspection immediately after contract execution, then adding specialty inspections only if the general report flags concerns. The goal is to have reports and estimates in hand well before your option deadline.

faqItems.q: 4) Do I need to worry about flood risk in New Braunfels or Canyon Lake?
faqItems.a: You should always check. Hill Country drainage can be very property-specific. We’ll review the seller’s disclosures, look at drainage patterns on-site, and verify flood information through FEMA’s map tools, then decide what additional due diligence makes sense.

faqItems.q: 5) How do I write a strong offer without overpaying?
faqItems.a: A strong offer is usually a clean offer: realistic price supported by recent comps, clear timelines, proof of funds, and terms that remove uncertainty for the seller (while still protecting you). The goal is to win the right house on the right terms, not to ‘win at any cost.’

title: Texas Veteran Home Buying Plan
excerpt: Texas veteran home buying plan for PCS and military families: VA loan strategy, cash-to-close expectations, commute fit, and smarter next steps.
content:
Moving to Texas as a veteran or military family, and trying to buy without making an expensive fast decision?

Quick Answer

A smart Texas veteran home buying plan starts before you tour homes. Confirm your VA entitlement and funding-fee status, build your budget around full monthly payment and cash-to-close, then narrow neighborhoods by commute, schools, and future flexibility before the PCS timeline gets tight.

If you want a veteran-focused plan for budget, timing, neighborhood fit, and offer strategy, Cody Posey Real Estate can help you map that out before you waste time on the wrong house or the wrong location.

The Complete Picture

If you are active duty, separating, retiring, or buying as a veteran after service, Texas can make a lot of sense. The problem is that many military buyers approach the move like a sprint. They get the pre-approval, pull up listings, and start trying to win a house before they have really answered the bigger questions: What monthly payment still feels comfortable after taxes and insurance? Which neighborhoods actually fit the duty-station commute? What school setup makes sense for this season? And if orders change again in a few years, will this home still be a smart hold, resale, or rental candidate?

That is where the process either gets calm or chaotic. VA financing is usually not the fragile part. The fragile part is trying to make big decisions too fast, especially when PCS timing, travel, temporary lodging, school enrollment, and daily-life logistics are all happening at once. The buyers who feel most in control are usually the ones who treat the purchase like a sequence of decisions, not one giant leap.

Official VA guidance still gives military buyers a strong edge in 2026. If you have full entitlement, VA.gov explains that you do not have a loan limit in the way most buyers assume, though the real ceiling still depends on lender approval, your income and debts, and the appraisal. That is good news, but it should not trick you into shopping at the top of what a lender says is possible. In real life, the best target is usually what keeps your payment manageable and your move flexible.

For Texas buyers, that payment-first approach matters even more. I tell people all the time that the listing price is only part of the story. Property taxes, homeowners insurance, and HOA dues can change the real affordability picture fast. If you are moving from a lower-tax state, that adjustment can feel bigger than expected. That does not mean Texas is a bad move. It just means the budget needs to be built correctly before you fall in love with a house.

Key Insights

If you want the cleanest path forward, focus on the decisions that remove uncertainty early. These are the areas that matter most for military and veteran buyers trying to move quickly without buying recklessly.

  • Confirm your VA benefit details before you build your search

    Your Certificate of Eligibility is not a minor admin step. It tells your lender how your entitlement stands today, and VA guidance makes it clear that full entitlement and partial entitlement create different planning realities. If you have full entitlement, the usual county-limit anxiety is mostly noise. If you have partial entitlement, the 2026 FHFA baseline limit matters much more because it can affect how much you can buy with zero down. Either way, clarity first is better than discovering a financing wrinkle after you are emotionally attached to a home.

    The funding fee question matters just as much. VA.gov says the purchase funding fee is 2.15% for first use with less than 5% down and 3.3% for subsequent use with less than 5% down, with lower percentages for larger down payments and exemptions for certain qualifying disability-related situations and some surviving spouses. That is not trivia. It changes your cash-to-close plan, your financed balance, and sometimes your confidence level. I would rather see a buyer confirm that at the start than untangle it in the last week before closing.

  • Shop by full payment and cash-to-close, not just price

    Military buyers are often told, correctly, that VA loans can be zero down. The mistake is hearing that as zero stress or zero cash needed. Inspections, earnest money, lender fees, prepaid items, taxes, insurance, and sometimes HOA setup costs still exist, and those numbers matter even more when you are also paying for a move, storage, travel, or short-term housing. A great lender quote should show you the whole picture, not just the loan amount.

    This is where the right-fit next step is usually a veteran-focused home buying plan, not more browsing. If we know your comfort-zone payment, expected cash-to-close, and timeline, we can point your search toward the right pockets from day one. If you are still in the early phase, start by browsing New Braunfels area listings to get a feel for price bands, then pressure-test the real payment before assuming any one home is a fit.

  • Neighborhood fit is really commute fit, school fit, and future-exit fit

    One of the most common mistakes military households make is treating every home in the broader San Antonio, New Braunfels, Cibolo, Schertz, Seguin, and Hill Country corridor as basically interchangeable. They are not. A home that looks perfect online can create a frustrating daily drive, an awkward school plan, or a maintenance burden that feels a lot less charming once the move settles down. Your routine matters more than the listing photos.

    That is why I like to filter neighborhoods through daily life first. Where is the duty station or civilian job? How much drive time feels sustainable? Are you trying to optimize for schools, access to medical care, quicker airport runs, newer construction, lower maintenance, or a future resale audience? If you want a clearer sense of how I think through local fit, learn about my approach and then we can narrow your shortlist based on your real routine, not generic hype.

  • Fast moves need cleaner homes and cleaner timelines

    The VA appraisal is not the enemy. In most cases, the real problem is a weak timeline or a property with avoidable condition issues. When a buyer is already working against travel deadlines, report dates, or family logistics, it usually makes sense to favor homes that are more likely to appraise and close cleanly. That means paying attention to obvious repair exposure, deferred maintenance, and anything that could trigger a repair debate right when you need the deal to stay on track.

    Older homes can absolutely still be the right move, and I am not saying veteran buyers should only buy new construction. I am saying the house and the timeline need to match. If the timeline is tight, the smartest house is often the one with fewer surprises. If the property is more complex, then the contract needs more breathing room. The mistake is mixing a high-friction property with a compressed schedule and hoping it all magically works out.

  • Do not ignore your next move while making this one

    A lot of military buyers make decisions as if this is the forever home, then orders change, priorities change, or the family needs change sooner than expected. The better question is not just, “Do we like this home?” It is also, “Will this still make sense if we need to sell, rent, or pivot in a few years?” A smart veteran home buying plan includes an exit strategy before you ever go under contract.

    That means thinking about neighborhood desirability, maintenance load, layout usefulness, and what the next buyer will care about later. Sometimes the right house is not the flashiest one. It is the one that protects your options. If you want help reading that tradeoff in plain English, Cody Posey Real Estate can walk you through the pocket-by-pocket pros and cons.

Market Reality

Here is the honest version of the 2026 market for military buyers in this part of Texas. Conditions are more negotiable than the peak frenzy years, but that does not mean every listing is a bargain or every seller is easy. Some homes still move quickly, especially when they are well-maintained, well-located, and priced cleanly. Other homes sit longer because buyers are more payment-sensitive and more selective than they were a few years ago. That is actually useful if you know how to read it.

For veteran buyers, a calmer market can create room to negotiate repairs, credits, or terms that reduce pressure on your cash-to-close. That matters when you are balancing a move and trying to keep reserves intact. But the biggest win is not just saving a few thousand dollars. The biggest win is reducing uncertainty. If a seller is willing to work with your timing or help with closing-cost structure, the whole transaction becomes easier to manage.

The flip side is that buyers can get lulled into thinking they have all the time in the world. Then they start touring too broadly, second-guessing every neighborhood, and waiting until the move is close before making real decisions. That is when stress spikes. A more balanced market still rewards clarity. When you know your payment, your preferred commute, your non-negotiables, and your timeline, you can act quickly without acting blindly.

One more reality check: “veteran-friendly” is not just about the loan type. It is about whether your team understands military timing, remote decision-making, and the practical difference between a house that looks good online and a house that supports your life. I think that is what buyers actually want, especially when they are moving fast. Not a motivational speech. Just a straightforward plan that helps them avoid the expensive mistakes.

Action Steps

  1. Get your lender to confirm three things this week: your COE status, your funding-fee status, and a realistic cash-to-close estimate that includes taxes, insurance, and HOA if applicable.
  2. Pick a true monthly comfort number. Not the maximum a lender says you can qualify for. The number that still feels stable if the move gets messy or life changes in 12 to 24 months.
  3. Build a neighborhood shortlist around routine. Start with commute targets, school priorities, maintenance tolerance, and your likely hold period. If you are still broad, explore local neighborhoods before you let listing photos drive the whole search.
  4. Match the house to the timeline. If your schedule is tight, lean toward homes with fewer obvious condition risks. If you want an older or more unique home, build extra time into your inspection and negotiation plan.
  5. Book a veteran-focused buying consultation before you write. The smartest time to pressure-test your budget, neighborhood fit, and offer structure is before you are emotionally committed. If you want that kind of plan, Cody Posey Real Estate is the right next step.

Closing

If you are a veteran, active-duty buyer, or military household planning a move in Texas, you do not need more noise. You need a clean plan that matches your budget, your timeline, and the way you actually live. That usually means clarifying the financing first, then choosing the right neighborhood pockets, then writing a contract that fits the move instead of fighting it.

If you want help building that plan, I can help you sort through commute tradeoffs, school and neighborhood fit, cash-to-close expectations, and the offer strategy that makes the most sense for your timeline. Start with Cody Posey Real Estate, and we will build a veteran-focused home buying plan around your next move.

Ready to talk strategy? Call Cody Posey Real Estate at 830.360.5569.

Sources: VA.gov, VA Home Loan Entitlement and Limits; VA.gov, Funding Fee and Loan Closing Costs; FHFA, 2026 Conforming Loan Limit Values; Texas Veterans Land Board.

faqItems.q: 1) Do VA loans still have limits in Texas in 2026?
faqItems.a: If you have full entitlement, VA.gov says you do not have a loan limit in the way most buyers mean it. Your actual ceiling still depends on lender approval, your income and debts, and the appraisal. If you have partial entitlement, county-limit math still matters.

faqItems.q: 2) How much cash do veteran buyers usually need if they are using a VA loan?
faqItems.a: It varies. Even with zero down, you still need to plan for earnest money, inspections, lender and title fees, prepaid items, and sometimes HOA-related costs. A good plan starts with a real lender estimate, not a headline promise.

faqItems.q: 3) What is the biggest mistake military buyers make during a PCS move?
faqItems.a: Usually it is compressing the whole process. They wait too long to clarify financing, then try to solve budget, neighborhood fit, tours, offer strategy, and closing logistics all at once.

faqItems.q: 4) Should I choose a neighborhood based mostly on resale or mostly on my current commute?
faqItems.a: Usually both matter, but the order depends on your hold period. If there is a realistic chance you will move again in a few years, look for neighborhoods that work for daily life now and still give you a reasonable exit later.

faqItems.q: 5) What is the right next step if I am early in the process?
faqItems.a: A veteran-focused home buying plan. That means confirming VA details, pressure-testing payment, narrowing the search to the right pockets, and deciding what kind of offer structure makes sense before you start reacting to listings.

title: New Braunfels Move Plan for Life Changes
excerpt: A practical New Braunfels move plan for downsizing, relocating, divorce, retirement, or a fresh start, with clear next steps for Hill Country buyers and sellers.
content:
How do you make a smart housing move when life changes first and the real estate decision comes second?

Quick Answer

In 2026, a life-change move in New Braunfels usually works best when you build the plan in this order: timeline, monthly budget, equity position, area fit, then home criteria. With Texas mortgage rates still around the mid-6% range and the broader San Antonio-New Braunfels market giving buyers more breathing room than the frenzy years, the people who move well are the ones who turn an emotional transition into a written strategy before they start chasing listings.

The Complete Picture

New Braunfels and the surrounding Hill Country still benefit from real migration and long-term demand. FRED’s U.S. Census metro series put the San Antonio-New Braunfels MSA at 2.813 million residents in 2025, and Texas Demographic Center data shows Comal and Guadalupe counties are still absorbing meaningful growth. That matters because even when the market cools from peak speed, the area keeps attracting retirees, relocating families, military households, and people looking for more space or a different pace of life.

At the same time, affordability is not magically easy. Bankrate listed Texas 30-year mortgage rates at 6.25% on April 24, 2026, and statewide housing supply near 5.5 months, which is close to balanced territory. In plain English, that means buyers often have more room to negotiate than they did in the low-inventory scramble, but payment shock is still real. A move that looks emotionally right can still become financially tight if the budget is built around the top of your approval range instead of your actual comfort range.

That is why lifestyle-change moves need a different lens than a generic market update. A downsizer is usually trying to reduce upkeep, preserve equity, and simplify. An upsizer may be solving for bedrooms, storage, school logistics, or multigenerational living. A relocator may care more about commute patterns, neighborhood feel, and whether new construction or resale gives the cleanest landing. Someone going through divorce or a fresh start often needs stability, discretion, and a plan that does not create more chaos than the move itself.

The core question is not just, "Can I buy or sell right now?" The better question is, "What kind of move creates the most stability for the next two to five years?" That is where a real plan beats a reactive search every time.

Key Insights

Most transition moves do not fail because the market is impossible. They fail because the decision gets made in the wrong order. Start with the life math, then let the real estate strategy support it.

  • Timing matters more when the move is emotional

    FRED’s median days-on-market series for the San Antonio-New Braunfels CBSA showed 61 days in March 2026. That gives many buyers and sellers more time than they had during the peak frenzy, but it should not be mistaken for unlimited flexibility. If you are relocating for work, finalizing a divorce, or trying to buy after selling a longtime home, the market can feel calm right up until your personal deadline becomes urgent. A written timeline with backup options usually matters more than trying to predict the perfect week to act. The people who stay calm tend to decide their sequence early, not late.

  • Budget clarity beats maximum approval

    A lender may tell you what you can qualify for, but that is not the same as what feels sustainable after a major life transition. Retirement, single-income living, or a bigger monthly payment after an upsizing move can change how a house feels six months after closing. In this rate environment, the safer move is usually to build from your desired monthly payment backward. That gives you a cleaner filter for taxes, insurance, HOA costs, and whether builder incentives or seller credits should be part of the plan. It also keeps you from buying a house that solves one problem while creating three new ones.

  • Area fit should come before house features

    New Braunfels, Canyon Lake, Seguin, Bulverde, and Cibolo-Schertz are not interchangeable. New Braunfels often gives you the strongest blend of lifestyle, river-town identity, and access to San Antonio and Austin corridors. Canyon Lake can appeal to buyers prioritizing scenery, lower-density living, and a different pace, but the drive patterns and home types are different. Seguin can make sense for buyers chasing value and space. Cibolo-Schertz is often more practical for San Antonio-area commuting households. If you pick the kitchen before you pick the daily life, you can end up in the wrong zip code with the right countertops.

  • Equity planning is a real strategy, not a side note

    For sellers, especially downsizers or longtime owners, the move is often funded by accumulated equity. That means pricing, repair strategy, and the net sheet matter more than the headline list price. For buyers selling first, the equity from the current home may shape the down payment, reserve cushion, or ability to buy down the rate. In a more balanced market, sloppy pricing or unclear expectations can erode that advantage fast. This is where a local strategy session with Cody Posey Real Estate is usually more valuable than another hour scrolling portals.

Market Reality

This is not the market of two years ago, and that is not a bad thing. Buyers are often seeing more options, more time for due diligence, and more room for negotiation on homes that are overpriced, dated, or competing with builder inventory. Sellers can still win, but not by pretending the market owes them 2021 terms. If the move is tied to a life event, accuracy matters more than optimism.

That is especially true in New Braunfels, where local demand still exists but buyers are more payment-sensitive. A family moving up may want more bedrooms, a yard, or proximity to a certain routine, but they are also comparing monthly payment, commute, and whether a slightly farther-out location makes the numbers work better. A retiree may love the idea of keeping a foothold in the Hill Country, but the better move may be reducing stairs, maintenance, and tax burden instead of stretching for the prettiest view.

Relocators into the area need to watch a different set of tradeoffs. New construction can offer incentives, warranties, and predictable condition, while resale can offer better lot character, established trees, proximity to downtown New Braunfels, Gruene, or river access, and a more lived-in neighborhood feel. Neither is automatically better. The right answer depends on whether the move is about speed, simplicity, budget control, future resale, or day-to-day lifestyle.

The emotional side also deserves some honesty. Divorce moves, fresh-start moves, and late-stage family transitions often create pressure to just get it done. I get the appeal, but rushed decisions are expensive. A rushed sale can leave money on the table. A rushed purchase can trap you in the wrong payment or the wrong area. Usually the smartest move is not the fastest move. It is the move that protects options while still getting you to stable ground.

Action Steps

  • Write down the actual life goal. Is this move about less upkeep, more space, a new school setup, a shorter commute, financial reset, or being closer to family? If the goal is blurry, the house search will be blurry too.
  • Set a payment comfort zone, not just a qualification ceiling. Include principal, interest, taxes, insurance, HOA, utilities, and moving costs. If you are between homes, also decide how much cash reserve you want left after closing.
  • Choose your top two or three areas before choosing listings. Use daily-life criteria like commute, access to healthcare, schools, walkability, lot size, maintenance level, and neighborhood feel. If you are early in the process, start with the New Braunfels market and home search resources and narrow from there.
  • Map the sequence. Decide whether you need to sell first, buy first, lease temporarily, or target a same-week transition. This one decision affects leverage, stress level, and how aggressive you can be on price.
  • Book a strategy conversation before touring too much. A short planning call can sort out timing, equity, pricing expectations, and area fit faster than ten random showings. If you are in a transition season, start with a direct strategy conversation with Cody Posey Real Estate so the plan is built around your situation instead of generic advice.

Next Best Step

If your move is tied to a life change, stop asking, "Should I wait?" and start asking, "What sequence gives me the cleanest next chapter?" If you want help pressure-testing the timeline, budget, and area options, Cody Posey Real Estate can help you build that move plan before you make a costly guess.

Closing

Big life changes do not need a perfect market. They need a clear plan. If you are trying to turn a transition into a smart next move in New Braunfels or the Hill Country, the goal is not to force certainty where life feels messy. The goal is to create enough structure that your next home supports the chapter you are actually stepping into.

Ready to talk strategy? Call Cody Posey Real Estate at 830.360.5569.

Sources: FRED, Housing Inventory: Median Days on Market in San Antonio-New Braunfels, TX (CBSA); FRED, Resident Population in San Antonio-New Braunfels, TX (MSA); Bankrate Texas Mortgage Rates; Texas Demographic Center, Demographic Trends in Texas and the San Antonio Region.

faqItems.q: Is 2026 a good time to make a lifestyle-change move in New Braunfels?
faqItems.a: It can be, especially for people who need flexibility more than speed. Inventory is more workable than during the peak frenzy, but financing costs still require a tighter budget strategy. If your life event is already driving the move, the better question is whether your timing and payment plan are ready.

faqItems.q: Should I buy first or sell first if I am downsizing?
faqItems.a: It depends on cash reserves, equity, and tolerance for carrying two homes at once. Many downsizers benefit from understanding their likely net proceeds first, because that shapes purchase price and stress level. A clean net sheet usually makes the rest of the decision easier.

faqItems.q: What areas around New Braunfels make sense for a fresh start?
faqItems.a: New Braunfels works well for buyers who want lifestyle, convenience, and broad resale appeal. Canyon Lake fits people prioritizing scenery and a slower pace. Seguin can offer more value per dollar. Cibolo-Schertz often makes sense for stronger San Antonio commuting patterns.

faqItems.q: How do higher rates affect an upsizing move?
faqItems.a: Higher rates usually make monthly payment the main pressure point, even if equity is strong. That means upsizers need to compare not just price, but taxes, insurance, and whether a builder incentive or seller credit changes the monthly math enough to matter.

faqItems.q: What is the biggest mistake people make during a transition move?
faqItems.a: They search for houses before they define the move. That usually leads to mixed signals, emotional overreaction, and choices that do not match the actual life need. The plan should come first, then the listings.
